title: Anomalia de  copywriting

# OPPORTUNITY SCORECARD: Anomalia de  copywriting
  
## 1. Mapeamento de Oportunidade
- **Problema de Mercado:** Usuários expressam frustrações profundas com soluções existentes para  copywriting.
- **Público Mapeado (ICP):** Pequenos negócios, freelancers e agências de marketing digital.
- **Intensidade da Dor (0-10):** 7
- **Viabilidade Técnica (0-10):** 8
- **Saturação Comercial (1-10):** 3
- **GOLD SCORE:** 18.67/10 (OPORTUNIDADE DE DOMÍNIO)

Title: You're The Boss

Published: 2025-03-28T17:10:07.000Z
Author: Katie
Highlights:
**It also opened my eyes to some problems with copywriting right now.**
...
# Why copywriting feels harder than ever right now (part 1):
...
# It’s an industry problem.
...
Copywriting has been watered down, and it is simply NOT FUN.
...
1. Tech companies beat real copywriting to death. And everything has been so homogenized in marketing, that nothing exists as an experience anymore.
...
2. It’s harder to find companies that want catchphrases that live forever. Everyone is using safe, sterile, AI-polished messaging. So they’ve shrunk the reasons to hire good writers.
...
# Why copywriting is less fun (part 2):
...
1. Hiring is not fun.
...
2. The easiest way to get copywriting jobs is to know someone, ask that someone, and bide time for referrals. (Not a fun answer. But it’s also fun to save yourself from spending money on copywriting courses that are dated with old advice from, like, 2022.)
...
3. THE WORK IS NOT FUN. I REPEAT, THE WORK IS NOT FUN.
...
4. Copywriting has become less of an experience because products and offers have become less of an experience (due to boring as fuck stakeholders beating a dead horse.) Therefore, copywriting overall has become a harder sell.
...
5. The entire process just isn’t fun. Meeting people on Zoom. Asking questions. Writing copy. Yes, it’s work. Work is work. But I think we’re starting to see some fatigue from remote work. \*\*\*\*
...
fatigue, especially from
...
1. In a bad economy that’s been stretched out for a long time now (\*gestures at American food prices and shitty President/Actually a criminal\*) …**People and companies are spending money differently.**
...
# It’s easier to get people to pay for FUN + harder to get people to pay for what’s good for them in the future
...
Do you know
...
single day? People paying to go on the go
...
.**When it
...
to having FUN, people are*****all in*****. The value is immediately clear—they want to have a good time.**
...
Even if the value of copywriting has been solidified for a while now (make more money), it seems that companies have gotten literally more stupid over time and “make more money” doesn’t seem to compute to them (as much as it does for SMBs.)
...
The promise of long term benefits like better conversions, more leads, stronger branding… \*YOU\* know how important it is, but it’s gotten harder to make the connection to show them what’s in it for them.
...
**And that means having really TIGHT messaging in your emails and the ability to speak well over calls.**
...
Sorry, but as a burnt out copywriter, that’s fucking exhausting. LOL. Meaning, unless it’s an easy referral sent my way, I no longer have the energy to try to play mind games with companies.

Title: I love copywriting. I loathe selling.
URL: https://lunacyletters.substack.com/p/i-love-copywriting-i-loathe-selling
Published: 2025-02-14T19:54:05.000Z
Author: Lauren
Highlights:
Even though I love copywriters and copywriting, I hate getting emails from copywriters trying to*sell*to me.
...
Undoubtedly (a very \*pinkies up\* word), there’s a copywriter with a few dozen blog posts on what copywriting is, why it’s important—and for some f\*cking reason why they switched to Showit, Showit vs. Squarespace, and what the best Showit templates are for 2025—that can tell you that copywriting*isn’t just*selling. It’s getting someone to take action, convert, reach the top, win the battle, get the boy, get the girl, and reach your glowiest potential.
...
And when I know you’re selling to me through known tactics, scripts, formulas, and repetitive time-pressure headlines like*GONE BY TONIGHT!! OMG!!,*I get the ick, and I never want to buy from you.
...
One copywriter I worship, admire, and want to watch a week-long Housewives marathon with is a huge culprit of giving me the selling ick. I’m dangerously close to popping open one of her emails and making this break-up official in the most obvious way possible: clicking Unsubscribe (and subsequently clicking ‘Confirm Unsubscribe’).
...
It’s not that I’m wise to what’s going on. I just see the same marketing tactics and copy approaches all the f\*cking time. It’s boring. And the embodiment of whatever that emoji is with closed eyes and a zig-zag mouth.
...
and away from the Converkits
...
some reason?) and Mailchimps.
...
I don’t want to sell. I want to write.
...
with*DOORS
...
ON THIS EXCLUSIVE OFFER!!! GET
...
! TIK TO
...
After five years of freelance copywriting, I realized that I hate writing about the same thing everyone else has written about.
...
When I was writing stuff like “Best Copy Practices for High ROI,” or some other SEO word salad, I’d look like that dutiful cardigan-wearing copywriter in a coffee shop tickling away at her keyboard with a soft breeze blowing through her perfect hair. In reality, I wanted to bang my head against a wall or just order another scone. Most of the time, my drafts wound up in Untitled Document purgatory. Alone, abandonded, periodless.
...
**lunacy letters**is my answer for that. Substack is my platform for that answer. I don’t want to sell —that’s the bottom line. I want to talk. That’s why I got off the email trash train.
...
small businesses and people who want to sell all the f\*cking time? Of course.
...
I’m not in that bucket. I have nothing to sell you (I mean, I do, if you’re in the market for copy or a better brand personality then[slide over here](https://themoongaze.com)). But ninety-nine percent of the time, I don’t want to lead you to a link to a landing page, to an offer, to a funnel, to a deal, and so on and so forth.

Title: you hate pain point marketing (but you're secretly doing pain point marketing)
URL: https://thetwelfthhouse.substack.com/p/you-hate-pain-point-marketing-but
Published: 2026-04-02T13:38:28.000Z
Author: Holisticism
Highlights:
Here’s what I want to say to this, gently, from the bottom of my caffeinated little heart: you do pain point marketing. You have always done pain point marketing. Every single person reading this, who’s promoted and sold anything ever does pain point marketing. And that is completely, 100% fine — because pain point marketing is NAHT the thing you actually have a problem with.
...
There is no marketing that doesn’t address a pain point. There is no human desire that doesn’t have a corresponding lack underneath it. Every piece of content that has ever moved anyone — including the soft, aesthetic, “I’m just sharing my life” kind — is operating on the gap between where someone is and where they want to be. A space between where the reader is right now and where they want to be. That gap might be a wound. It might be frustration. It might just be curiosity, or an itch, or the low-grade awareness that something could be better than it currently is. But there is a point of tension, and that tension is the reason anyone keeps reading past the first sentence.
...
What people are reacting to — correctly, with their whole chest, and I am right there with them — is coercion. Coercive marketing has become so synonymous with “paint point marketing” that we mistake them for the same thing. But it doesn’t have to be that way. Despite what people on the internet say, you can do marketing without being a manipulative asshole.
...
Non-coercive marketing names a real problem the reader already recognizes in themselves. It says: here’s the gap I see, here’s what I think is going on, here’s what I built to help. The reader goes “oh, yeah, that is my situation” — not because you convinced them it was, but because it already was before they opened your email. And then non-coercive marketing
...
make the best choice for themselves. It’s not my job to convince you, or manipulate you, or, idk, put you in some NLP Jedi mind trick space
...
The difference in
...
pain. The difference is
...
you use the
...
pain against them.
...
“sales-
...
” — the stuff that made you cringe so hard you decided
...
opt out of
...
entirely — is the aggressive, manipulative, countdown-timer-
...
-webinar stuff.
...
comes to mind
...
,” and once
...
You are doing pain point marketing. You have always been doing pain point marketing. Every marketer who has ever moved someone to act — including the gentle ones, including the heart-centered ones, including the ones who would rather eat broken glass than use a countdown timer — has done so by addressing a gap between where their reader is and where their reader wants to be. That gap is a pain point. It just doesn’t always look like a wound.

Title: “The Workaround”: An alternative to pushy sales copy | HarrisonAmy Copywriting
URL: https://harrisonamy.com/workaround-copywriting-technique/
Published: 2017-07-26T17:31:58.000Z
Author: Amy Harrison
Highlights:
“The Workaround”: An alternative to pushy sales copy | HarrisonAmy Copywriting
...
As a result,
...
can write copy that suggests our product or service is the
...
way to do things,
...
# ‘The Workaround’ copywriting technique
...
The Workaround is when you address the fact that an ideal prospect knows they have a problem, is trying to solve that problem, but isn’t using you. In this advert, Regus are asking the question:
...
What other methods are prospects using to try and solve the problem we solve?
...
The ‘try’ part is important. You want to highlight a method that is, of course, inferior to yours. You don’t want to highlight something that works better than what you have (as if that’s possible!)
...
So in this example, we have someone looking for an affordable way to run a business and using cafes and free workspaces to do so. This solves the problem of keeping costs low. Unfortunately it comes with pitfalls that could create more danger to them, including harm to their reputation plus additional annoyances of noise, interruptions, etc.
...
# The Workaround Formula
...
To use the workaround in your copywriting, I recommend building out the following narrative points in your notes:
...
1. It’s just a terrible idea…
2. That makes sense!
3. You might be using this method to solve it
4. Here’s a problem you face
...
Which, without the conversational aspect, translates to:
...
1. Dangers of workaround
2. Logical reasoning for workaround
3. Workaround
4. Problem
...
If we look at the above formula we can see:
...
1. Dangers – Potential collapse
2. Logical Reasoning – Materials to hand, cheaper and faster than hiring a professional, (plus permission from parents)
3. Workaround – Build bunkbeds (themselves)
4. Problem – Lack of space
...
What you need to do is identify your prospect’s workaround, then build a compelling argument that shows you understand why they are doing it this way, but then point out the hidden dangers. Here’s a quick example crib sheet that we might build for a business that provides invoicing software for small businesses.
...
To build your own, simply answer the following questions:
...
1. What are the potential dangers of doing this?
2. Why does this make sense?
3. How are they trying to do this?
4. What problem does your prospect want to solve?
...
If you build these pieces and use them in your copy, it’s an alternative to the marketing rugby tackle. Instead you meet prospects where they are and guide them to your (much better, less dangerous) solution.
...
What would you discover by answering the above questions? What workaround is your prospect using, and what are the dangers of them continuing down this path? Let me know in the comments!

---

Title: Ep 42: Kill the Workaround - Show Customers that NOT Using You is a Bad Idea - Write With Influence -
URL: https://writewithinfluence.com/podcast/write-with-influence-podcast/ep-42-kill-the-workaround-show-customers-that-not-using-you-is-a-bad-idea/
Published: 2021-07-23T12:01:39.000Z
Author: amy harrison
Highlights:
So, you want to acknowledge what they’re doing and also acknowledge why it makes sense but then we need to introduce component number three, which is: Why what they’re doing is really a bad idea. Now this doesn’t have to be dramatic. It’s not like if they keep doing what they’re doing, the world will end. Your consequences have to be believable, but it should be uncomfortable. If you can point to specific costs or risks of them following the approach that they’re following and if you can even show why it’s not going to get them the results that they think, then this is really good material for your copy – can you point to time lost by doing something manually? Or perhaps there’s a risk that they don’t know about that they’re exposing themselves to by taking a shortcut? I do see this a lot in larger organizations that may be, as I mentioned, manually tracking and managing things in spreadsheets, but spreadsheets can go missing, files can get corrupted, and a risk of this may be that they don’t have the information that they need to be compliant or to face an audit. So think about the potential downfall of what they’re doing. Will it be more expensive in the long run to fix what they’re doing today? Pretty much anything I tried to do myself DIY-wise is going to cost me more to fix, which is why I don’t do it!
...
Finally, point number four is, we want to contrast this consequence and this risk with what we have to offer. And it’s important to remember that when you do this, you also need to tackle the appeal of the shortcut and that means tackling point number two: Why did it make sense to them in the first place to look for free information e.g., handle things in spreadsheets, etc. It’s like the course I bought, the creator points out that, yes, you can find YouTube tutorials and that makes sense because it’s free and there’s some excellent tutorials out there, but you will spend more time searching for answers than learning practical skills and putting them into action. And with it being a 14 day guided course broken down into short lessons, it’s something that you could fit into a schedule and actually use, compared to a long list of YouTube videos that you might click save, save, save, but never get around to watching. So for your customer, can you show them that not only will they get better results by doing what it is that you have, but that it’s actually easier, faster, cheaper, and less disruptive to take your solution than they may have previously thought.
...
1. What are they doing?
2. Why does that make sense to them?
3. Why is it a bad idea?
4. Why is your solution better for them and easier than they think?

---

Title: LLM Limitations & Workarounds 2026: 8 Key Constraints
URL: https://www.promptquorum.com/prompt-engineering/ai-limitations-what-llms-cant-do
Published: 2026-03-24T04:48:54.000Z
Author: Hans Kuepper
Highlights:
The primary workaround is retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), which injects live or recent documents into the prompt at query time. A secondary workaround is prompt grounding: pasting the relevant current facts directly into the prompt and instructing the model to answer only from that context.
...
Workarounds: provide the source material in the prompt and instruct the model to answer only from it; ask the model to flag any claim it cannot confirm from provided context; use RAG to anchor answers to verified documents; validate all key figures against primary sources before publishing.
...
- Hallucination is a feature for generative tasks. The same token-prediction mechanism that fabricates citations also generates novel metaphors, product names, and creative variations that no retrieval system could produce. Designers, copywriters, and product teams often want LLM "confabulation" — the problem arises only when treating generated content as factual. Separating generation tasks from fact-lookup tasks eliminates most hallucination risk without suppressing creativity.
...
Two of the most effective techniques for compensating for these limitations are chain-of-thought prompting — which externalises reasoning steps and reduces errors — and RAG, which compensates for knowledge cutoffs by retrieving fresh context. See chain-of-thought prompting and RAG explained.
...
Provide grounding context explicitly. Paste in the relevant facts, documents, or data the model needs. Never assume the model has current, accurate, or domain-specific knowledge.
...
Instruct the model to signal uncertainty. Add a line like: "If you are not certain about a specific fact, say so explicitly rather than guessing." Models comply
...
a higher rate than they hallucinate spontaneously.
...
Validate outputs before publishing. Check all key figures, dates, citations, and technical specifications against authoritative primary sources. LLM output is a high-quality draft, not a primary source.
...
- RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation)** — Architecture where relevant documents are retrieved from an external
...
base and injected into the prompt at query time. The primary workaround for
...
cutoffs.
...
A bug is an unintended error fixable with a software update. A limitation is a structural property of how the model works. Hallucination, knowledge cutoffs, and context window caps are limitations — they emerge from the transformer architecture and training process and cannot be patched away, only worked around with system design.

Title: Patentformer: A demonstration of AI-assisted automated patent drafting
URL: https://arxiv.org/html/2510.09752v1
Published: N/A
Author: N/A
Highlights:
In this paper, we provide demonstration of Patentformer Wang et al. (2024), a novel system designed to generate patent specifications, and address the challenges outlined earlier. Patentformer operates by taking a patent claim and any associated drawing text as input. The system first preprocesses and enhances the input to improve its readability and structure, facilitating a better comprehension by the LL
...
. The enhanced text is then passed to a fine-tuned LLM, which has been specifically trained on publicly available patent data to learn the
...
The objective of Patentformer algorithm Wang et al. (2024) is to generate detailed rich text based on the provided textual information. Formally, let 𝒫\mathcal{P} represent a patent document consisting of:
...
The task is to generate the output specification 𝒮\mathcal{S} using the claims 𝒞\mathcal{C}, the drawing descriptions ℬ\mathcal{B}, and the component name-number pairs 𝒩\mathcal{N} as inputs. The generated specification must: i) incorporate and support all the features present in the claims 𝒞\mathcal{C}, ii) accurately describe the drawings using the drawing descriptions ℬ\mathcal{B}, and iii) correctly reference the components in the drawings by utilizing the component name-number pairs in 𝒩\mathcal{N}. This process is formally expressed as 𝒯→𝒮\mathcal{T}\rightarrow\mathcal{S}, where 𝒯\mathcal{T} represents the combined input information {𝒞,ℬ,𝒩}\{\mathcal{C},\mathcal{B},\mathcal{N}\} used to generate the output specification 𝒮\mathcal{S}. Instead of using the plain text for the training tuples, e.g., 𝒯\mathcal{T} = (𝒞\mathcal{C}, ℬ\mathcal{B}, 𝒩\mathcal{N}), Patentformer designs a rich version of 𝒯\mathcal{T} as 𝒯′\mathcal{T^{\prime}} = (𝒞′\mathcal{C^{\prime}}, ℬ′\mathcal{B^{\prime}}, 𝒩′\mathcal{N^{\prime}}), and generates the processed 𝒮′\mathcal{S^{\prime}}. That is, the Patentformer targets the task of 𝒯′→𝒮′\mathcal{T^{\prime}}\rightarrow\mathcal{S^{\prime}}, instead of 𝒯→𝒮\mathcal{T}\rightarrow\mathcal{S}.
...
We developed the Patentformer system, which utilizes the Patentformer algorithm, and deployed a user-interactive version capable of automatically generating and outputting patent-style text based on user inputs. To generate the final output specifications, the Patentformer system follows these three steps: 1. Input the claims and extract the drawing text from Visio drawings. 2. Map the figure and its components to the corresponding claims. 3. The underlying LLM generates specifications based on the input claims and drawing text pairs. Figure 2 illustrates the workflow of the Patentformer system.

Title: FlowPlan-G2P: A Structured Generation Framework for Transforming Scientific Papers into Patent Descriptions
URL: https://arxiv.org/html/2601.02589v4
Published: N/A
Author: N/A
Highlights:
Generating patent descriptions from scientific papers is challenging due to fundamental rhetorical and structural disparities between the two genres. Existing approaches treat this as surface-level rewriting, failing to capture the hierarchical reasoning and statutory constraints inherent in patent drafting. We propose FlowPlan-G2P, a graph-mediated generation framework that decomposes this transformation into three stages: (1) Concept Graph Induction, extracting technical entities and functional dependencies into a directed graph; (2) Section-level Planning, partitioning the graph into coherent subgraphs aligned with canonical patent sections; and (3) Graph-Conditioned Generation, synthesizing legally compliant paragraphs conditioned on section-specific subgraphs. Experiments on expert-validated benchmarks reveal that standard NLG metrics systematically favor legally non-compliant outputs over valid patent descriptions, motivating our domain-specific evaluation. Under this evaluation, FlowPlan-G2P with an open-weight backbone consistently outperforms vanilla proprietary models, demonstrating that structured decomposition is a stronger determinant of quality than model scale.
...
From the reasoning outputs $\{R_{i}\}$ , we extract patent elements as graph nodes $V$ and their inter-element relations as edges $E$ , forming a directed concept graph $G=(V,E)$ . Each node corresponds to a single functional unit: an atomic technical component that serves a distinct role within the invention’s architecture. The extraction granularity is governed by category-specific scoping: Field yields a single domain descriptor, TechProblem yields discrete limitation statements of prior art, Solution targets core algorithms or functional modules, Implementation targets concrete procedural steps, and Effects targets measurable outcomes. The complete scoping criteria for all nine categories are operationalized through the expert prompts provided in Appendix A. Edges encode functional or causal dependencies drawn from the following relation types:
...
The second stage reorganizes $G^{*}$ into legally compliant patent sections characterized by coherent paragraph-level flow. We construct a plan $\mathcal{P}=(S,T)$ , where $S$ denotes a set of section-specific subgraphs and $T$ represents their global order, which operates at two levels: (i) the canonical patent section ordering (Field $\rightarrow$ Background $\rightarrow$ Summary $\rightarrow$ Detailed Description $\rightarrow$ Effects), and (ii) the intra-section logical flow constraint (Problem $\rightarrow$ Solution $\rightarrow$ Implementation $\rightarrow$ Effects).

---

Title: Pap2Pat: Towards Automated Paper-to-Patent Drafting using Chunk-based Outline-guided Generation
URL: https://arxiv.org/html/2410.07009v1
Published: 2024-10-09T00:00:00.000Z
Author: N/A
Highlights:
A major challenge of the proposed task is the document length: patent descriptions in Pap2Pat are on average 18k tokens long, some exceeding 180k tokens. We propose chunk-based outline-guided generation, a novel approach to generating long patent documents chunk-by-chunk, guided by a user-defined outline and grounded in the corresponding research paper. We benchmark current open-weight LLMs on this task in both zero-shot and fine-tuning setups. Our main contributions are:
...
}}$ is proportional to the length of the text in that section ( $n_{\text{chars}}$ ).
...
In this section, we propose chunk-based outline-guided generation, a novel approach to patent generation illustrated in Figure 2. It serves as a strong baseline for comparison in future work. It is motivated by current LLMs’ limitations regarding sequence length (which needs to cover the instruction, the context, and the output) and therefore generates the patent in chunks, guided by the user-defined outline. We chunk the outline, retrieve paper context based on the outline chunk, and prompt an LLM to generate the patent text for that chunk. Finally, we concatenate the generated outputs and apply only a lightweight post-processing to remove duplicate headings at chunk boundaries.
...
Retriever. For each chunk $i$ , the outline $\text{o}_{i}$ is passed to a retriever, which selects relevant paragraphs from the paper to create the paper context $\text{c}_{i}$ . We use BM25 (Robertson and Zaragoza 2009) as the retrieval method and the chunk’s outline as the query. We always include the abstract of the paper because it provides a valuable overview, and successively add paragraphs in the order of their relevance ranking until the token limit is reached. The results are formatted in a Markdown document that comprises all headings and the retrieved paragraphs.
...
LLMs. The paper context $\text{c}_{i}$ , the current outline $\text{o}_{i}$ and prior outlines $\text{o}_{j<i}$ are combined to form the prompt to the LLM (see Appendix F). Based on this prompt, the LLM generates the patent chunk $\text{p}_{i}$ , where constrained decoding ensures that the model adheres strictly to the headings in the outline. To make our work reproducible, we only leverage recently published open-weights LLMs that have been reported to achieve state-of-the-art results on other generation tasks. We include a small model (Llama-3 8B (Dubey et al. 2024)) and several larger models (Mixtral-8x7B (Jiang et al. 2024), Llama-3 70B (Dubey et al. 2024) and Qwen2-72B (Yang et al. 2024)). Per default, we use a maximum sequence length of 8k tokens for all LLMs to ensure that the results are comparable and leave the study of the effect of increased maximum sequence length to future work.
